content.problemStatement: Write a program that Accepts the count of test cases t, then for each test case, reads an integer num, checks if it's even using the isEven function, and outputs "Even" or "Odd" accordingly
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer num.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print "Even" if num is even, otherwise print "Odd". Each answer is printed on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 2 and 6 are divisible by 2, therefore "Even". 5 leaves a remainder of 1, therefore "Odd".
Both 10 and 20 are multiples of 2, so the answer for each test case is "Even".
7 is not divisible by 2, hence the output is "Odd".

hidden.label: maximum boundary
hidden.expectedOutput:
Even
Even
Even
Even
Even
Even
Even
Even
Even
Even

hidden.label: stress/general
hidden.expectedOutput:
Odd
Even
Odd
Even
Odd
Even
Odd

solution.approach: The parity of a number can be determined by checking the remainder when divided by 2. If num % 2 equals 0 the number is even, otherwise it is odd. For each test case we apply this constant‑time check and output the corresponding word. The algorithm runs in O(T) time and uses O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) total, O(1) per test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few scalar variables are used.

code.Python3:
import sys

def is_even(num):
    return num%2==0

data=sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
if not data:
    sys.exit()
T=int(data[0])
out=[]
idx=1
for _ in range(T):
    num=int(data[idx]); idx+=1
    out.append('Even' if is_even(num) else 'Odd')
print('\n'.join(out))
